subject: Chemistry
text: IMPORTANT POINTS In haloarenes electrophilic substitution reaction occurs at o-& p-position. Haloarenes exhibit nucleophilic substitution reaction at extremely slow rate due to: Partial double bond character of C-X bond. Benzene ring is electron rich. Phenyl carbocation is not stable. More electronegativity of sp 2 hybridised carbon in haloarene. Steric hindrance due to benzene ring.
text_html:
IMPORTANT POINTS

  • In haloarenes electrophilic substitution reaction occurs at o-& p-position.
  • Haloarenes exhibit nucleophilic substitution reaction at extremely slow rate due to:
    1. Partial double bond character of C-X bond.
    2. Benzene ring is electron rich.
    3. Phenyl carbocation is not stable.
    4. More electronegativity of sp2 hybridised carbon in haloarene.
    5. Steric hindrance due to benzene ring.

subject: Chemistry
text: Common versus IUPAC Nomenclature The common name of alkyl halides is derived from the alkyl group name followed by halide. The IUPAC system calls them halosubstituted hydrocarbons.
text_html:
Common versus IUPAC Nomenclature
The common name of alkyl halides is derived from the alkyl group name followed by halide. The IUPAC system calls them halosubstituted hydrocarbons.

subject: Chemistry
text: Dihaloalkanes Naming In the common system, same-type halogen atom dihaloalkanes are called alkylidene or alkylene dihalides. The IUPAC names them as dihaloalkanes.
text_html:
Dihaloalkanes Naming
In the common system, same-type halogen atom dihaloalkanes are called alkylidene or alkylene dihalides. The IUPAC names them as dihaloalkanes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Geminal Halides Geminal halides have both halogen atoms on the same carbon atom. In the common naming system, they are known as alkylidene halides.
text_html:
Geminal Halides
Geminal halides have both halogen atoms on the same carbon atom. In the common naming system, they are known as alkylidene halides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Vicinal Halides Vicinal halides feature halogen atoms on adjacent carbon atoms. In the common system, they are called alkylene dihalides.
text_html:
Vicinal Halides
Vicinal halides feature halogen atoms on adjacent carbon atoms. In the common system, they are called alkylene dihalides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Common Halides Methyl chloride - Structure: {tex} {CH}_3 {Cl} {/tex} - IUPAC: Chloromethane n-Propyl fluoride - Structure: {tex} {CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {~F} {/tex} - IUPAC: 1-Fluoropropane Carbon tetrachloride - Structure: {tex} {CCl}_4 {/tex} - IUPAC: Tetrachloromethane
text_html:
Common Halides

  • Methyl chloride - Structure: {tex} {CH}_3 {Cl} {/tex} - IUPAC: Chloromethane
  • n-Propyl fluoride - Structure: {tex} {CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {~F} {/tex} - IUPAC: 1-Fluoropropane
  • Carbon tetrachloride - Structure: {tex} {CCl}_4 {/tex} - IUPAC: Tetrachloromethane

subject: Chemistry
text: Mono Halogen Substituted Derivatives Mono halogen substituted derivatives of benzene have identical common and IUPAC names.
text_html:
Mono Halogen Substituted Derivatives
Mono halogen substituted derivatives of benzene have identical common and IUPAC names.

subject: Chemistry
text: Dihalogen Derivatives Prefixes For dihalogen derivatives, the prefixes used are: Common system: o- (ortho), m- (meta), p- (para) IUPAC system: Numerals 1,2; 1,3; 1,4
text_html:
Dihalogen Derivatives Prefixes
For dihalogen derivatives, the prefixes used are:
  • Common system: o- (ortho), m- (meta), p- (para)
  • IUPAC system: Numerals 1,2; 1,3; 1,4

subject: Chemistry
text: o-Chlorotoluene In the common system, o-chlorotoluene denotes 1-chloro-2-methylbenzene in the IUPAC system.
text_html:
o-Chlorotoluene
In the common system, o-chlorotoluene denotes 1-chloro-2-methylbenzene in the IUPAC system.

subject: Chemistry
text: Benzyl Chloride Benzyl chloride corresponds to chlorophenylmethane under IUPAC nomenclature.
text_html:
Benzyl Chloride
Benzyl chloride corresponds to chlorophenylmethane under IUPAC nomenclature.

subject: Chemistry
text: Understanding Ortho, Meta, Para The prefixes indicate position: Ortho (o-): Substituents are adjacent or 1,2-position Meta (m-): Substituents are one carbon apart or 1,3-position Para (p-): Substituents are opposite or 1,4-position
text_html:
Understanding Ortho, Meta, Para
The prefixes indicate position:
  • Ortho (o-): Substituents are adjacent or 1,2-position
  • Meta (m-): Substituents are one carbon apart or 1,3-position
  • Para (p-): Substituents are opposite or 1,4-position

subject: Chemistry
text: Illustration of Substitution Positions Images displaying the ortho, meta, and para positions on a benzene ring assist in understanding substitution types.
text_html:
Illustration of Substitution Positions
Images displaying the ortho, meta, and para positions on a benzene ring assist in understanding substitution types.

subject: Chemistry
text: Common vs. IUPAC Naming Conventions While common and IUPAC names are the same for mono-substituted derivatives, they vary for dihalogen derivatives.
text_html:
Common vs. IUPAC Naming Conventions
While common and IUPAC names are the same for mono-substituted derivatives, they vary for dihalogen derivatives.

subject: Chemistry
text: Substitution Patterns on Benzene Substitution patterns in benzene affect the chemical and physical properties of the resultant compound.
text_html:
Substitution Patterns on Benzene
Substitution patterns in benzene affect the chemical and physical properties of the resultant compound.

subject: Chemistry
text: Examples of Naming Benzene Derivatives Examples, like o-chlorotoluene and benzyl chloride, help clarify the distinction between common and IUPAC names.
text_html:
Examples of Naming Benzene Derivatives
Examples, like o-chlorotoluene and benzyl chloride, help clarify the distinction between common and IUPAC names.

subject: Chemistry
text: Application of Nomenclature in Organic Chemistry Applying these naming conventions in organic chemistry is crucial for accurate communication and documentation of chemical compounds.
text_html:
Application of Nomenclature in Organic Chemistry
Applying these naming conventions in organic chemistry is crucial for accurate communication and documentation of chemical compounds.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Electronegativity and Polarization Halogen atoms are more electronegative than carbon. This results in the carbon-halogen bond of alkyl halides being polarised. The carbon atom bears a partial positive charge. The halogen atom bears a partial negative charge.
text_html:
Electronegativity and Polarization

Halogen atoms are more electronegative than carbon.

This results in the carbon-halogen bond of alkyl halides being polarised.

The carbon atom bears a partial positive charge.

The halogen atom bears a partial negative charge.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Size of Halogen Atoms Fluorine atom is the smallest in the group. Iodine atom is the largest.
text_html:
Size of Halogen Atoms
  • Fluorine atom is the smallest in the group.
  • Iodine atom is the largest.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Carbon-Halogen Bond Length The carbon-halogen bond length increases as you go down the group from C-F to C-I.
text_html:
Carbon-Halogen Bond Length

The carbon-halogen bond length increases as you go down the group from C-F to C-I.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Bond Lengths and Enthalpies: Methane Derivatives Bond Length (in pm): {tex}{CH}_3-{F}{/tex} : 139 {tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex} : 178 {tex}{CH}_3-{Br}{/tex} : 193 {tex}{CH}_3-{I}{/tex} : 214 Bond Enthalpies (in {tex} {kJmol}^{-1} {/tex} ): {tex}{CH}_3-{F}{/tex} : 452 {tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex} : 351 {tex}{CH}_3-{Br}{/tex} : 293 {tex}{CH}_3-{I}{/tex} : 234 Dipole Moments (in Debye): {tex}{CH}_3-{F}{/tex} : 1.847 {tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex} : 1.860 {tex}{CH}_3-{Br}{/tex} : 1.830 {tex}{CH}_3-{I}{/tex} : 1.636
text_html:
Bond Lengths and Enthalpies: Methane Derivatives

  • Bond Length (in pm):
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{F}{/tex}: 139
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex}: 178
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{Br}{/tex}: 193
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{I}{/tex}: 214
  • Bond Enthalpies (in {tex} {kJmol}^{-1} {/tex}):
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{F}{/tex}: 452
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex}: 351
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{Br}{/tex}: 293
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{I}{/tex}: 234
  • Dipole Moments (in Debye):
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{F}{/tex}: 1.847
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex}: 1.860
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{Br}{/tex}: 1.830
    • {tex}{CH}_3-{I}{/tex}: 1.636

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Trends in Dipole Moments The dipole moments of alkyl halides differ despite varying bond lengths. {tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex} has the highest dipole moment among the group.
text_html:
Trends in Dipole Moments

The dipole moments of alkyl halides differ despite varying bond lengths.

{tex}{CH}_3-{Cl}{/tex} has the highest dipole moment among the group.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Trends in Bond Lengths As the size of the halogen atom increases from fluorine to iodine, the bond length increases.
text_html:
Trends in Bond Lengths

As the size of the halogen atom increases from fluorine to iodine, the bond length increases.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Trends in Bond Enthalpies The bond enthalpies decrease as bond length increases from C-F to C-I.
text_html:
Trends in Bond Enthalpies

The bond enthalpies decrease as bond length increases from C-F to C-I.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Preparation of Alkyl Halides Alkyl halides can be effectively prepared from alcohols.
text_html:
Preparation of Alkyl Halides

Alkyl halides can be effectively prepared from alcohols.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Characteristic Bond Analysis Bond enthalpies are highest for C-F and decrease towards C-I as the bond lengths increase.
text_html:
Characteristic Bond Analysis

Bond enthalpies are highest for C-F and decrease towards C-I as the bond lengths increase.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloalkanes
text: Most Electronegativity Fluorine is the most electronegative element in the group, resulting in the strongest C-F bond.
text_html:
Most Electronegativity

Fluorine is the most electronegative element in the group, resulting in the strongest C-F bond.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Bond Type: Polar Covalent The C-X bond in haloarenes, like chlorobenzene and bromobenzene, is polar covalent. Halogens such as Cl and Br are more electronegative than carbon, resulting in a partial negative charge on the halogen and a partial positive charge on the carbon.
text_html:
Bond Type: Polar Covalent
The C-X bond in haloarenes, like chlorobenzene and bromobenzene, is polar covalent. Halogens such as Cl and Br are more electronegative than carbon, resulting in a partial negative charge on the halogen and a partial positive charge on the carbon.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Bond Strength and Length - Haloarenes have a shorter and stronger C-X bond compared to haloalkanes. - Due to resonance and partial double bond character, this makes the haloarene bond distinctively strong and short.
text_html:
Bond Strength and Length
- Haloarenes have a shorter and stronger C-X bond compared to haloalkanes.
- Due to resonance and partial double bond character, this makes the haloarene bond distinctively strong and short.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Resonance Effect The lone pair of electrons on the halogen can delocalize into the pi-electron system of the benzene ring, leading to resonance. This interaction gives rise to resonance structures with partial double bond character, making the C-X bond stronger and shorter.
text_html:
Resonance Effect
The lone pair of electrons on the halogen can delocalize into the pi-electron system of the benzene ring, leading to resonance. This interaction gives rise to resonance structures with partial double bond character, making the C-X bond stronger and shorter.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: sp2 Hybridization of Carbon - In haloarenes, the carbon atom bonded to the halogen is sp2 hybridized. - Such sp2 carbons hold their electrons closer to the nucleus than sp3 carbons, further strengthening and shortening the C-X bond.
text_html:
sp2 Hybridization of Carbon
- In haloarenes, the carbon atom bonded to the halogen is sp2 hybridized.
- Such sp2 carbons hold their electrons closer to the nucleus than sp3 carbons, further strengthening and shortening the C-X bond.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Low Reactivity Towards Nucleophilic Substitution Haloarenes exhibit low reactivity in nucleophilic substitution reactions due to: Resonance stabilization, which makes the C-X bond resistant to breaking. Partial double bond character adds to bond strength. Aromaticity is energetically unfavorable to disrupt.
text_html:
Low Reactivity Towards Nucleophilic Substitution
Haloarenes exhibit low reactivity in nucleophilic substitution reactions due to:
  • Resonance stabilization, which makes the C-X bond resistant to breaking.
  • Partial double bond character adds to bond strength.
  • Aromaticity is energetically unfavorable to disrupt.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Comparison: C-X Bond in Haloarenes vs. Haloalkanes Haloarenes: Shorter and stronger bonds due to resonance and hybridization effects. Haloalkanes: Longer and weaker bonds, lacking these resonating features.
text_html:
Comparison: C-X Bond in Haloarenes vs. Haloalkanes
  • Haloarenes: Shorter and stronger bonds due to resonance and hybridization effects.
  • Haloalkanes: Longer and weaker bonds, lacking these resonating features.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Electron Delocalization in Haloarenes The delocalization of electrons in haloarenes involves interaction between the lone pairs on the halogen and the pi-electron cloud of the benzene. This adds an element of stability due to resonance.
text_html:
Electron Delocalization in Haloarenes
The delocalization of electrons in haloarenes involves interaction between the lone pairs on the halogen and the pi-electron cloud of the benzene. This adds an element of stability due to resonance.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Resonance Structures in Haloarenes In haloarenes, potential resonance structures involve electron delocalization across the benzene ring, evident in structures like Ph-X ⟷ Ph^δ+=X^δ-.
text_html:
Resonance Structures in Haloarenes
In haloarenes, potential resonance structures involve electron delocalization across the benzene ring, evident in structures like Ph-X ⟷ Ph^δ+=X^δ-.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Aromaticity and Reactivity Haloarenes maintain aromaticity, thus resisting reactions that disrupt the stable benzene ring. This aromatic nature often poses a kinetic barrier to reactions.
text_html:
Aromaticity and Reactivity
Haloarenes maintain aromaticity, thus resisting reactions that disrupt the stable benzene ring. This aromatic nature often poses a kinetic barrier to reactions.

subject: Chemistry
topic: Nature of C-X Bond of Haloarenes
text: Haloarenes Characteristics In summary, haloarenes exhibit distinct features due to their aromatic structure: Enhanced C-X bond strength from resonance and hybridization. Low substitution reaction rates due to resonance-enhanced stability. Aromaticity preservation offering energetic stability.
text_html:
Haloarenes Characteristics
In summary, haloarenes exhibit distinct features due to their aromatic structure:
  • Enhanced C-X bond strength from resonance and hybridization.
  • Low substitution reaction rates due to resonance-enhanced stability.
  • Aromaticity preservation offering energetic stability.

subject: Chemistry
text: From Alcohols The hydroxyl group in alcohols is replaced by a halogen using halogen acids, phosphorus halides, or thionyl chloride. Thionyl chloride is preferred as it leaves by-products $$ {SO}_2 $$ and HCl, which are gaseous and escape easily.
text_html:
From Alcohols
The hydroxyl group in alcohols is replaced by a halogen using halogen acids, phosphorus halides, or thionyl chloride. Thionyl chloride is preferred as it leaves by-products $$ {SO}_2 $$ and HCl, which are gaseous and escape easily.

subject: Chemistry
text: Catalysts in Alcohol Halogenation For primary and secondary alcohols reacting with HCl, a catalyst $$ {ZnCl}_2 $$ is necessary. Tertiary alcohols can undergo the reaction at room temperature simply by shaking with concentrated HCl.
text_html:
Catalysts in Alcohol Halogenation
For primary and secondary alcohols reacting with HCl, a catalyst $$ {ZnCl}_2 $$ is necessary. Tertiary alcohols can undergo the reaction at room temperature simply by shaking with concentrated HCl.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alkyl Bromide Preparation Alkyl bromide is prepared by constant boiling with HBr (48%). For alkyl iodide, heat alcohols with sodium or potassium iodide in 95% ortho-phosphoric acid.
text_html:
Alkyl Bromide Preparation
Alkyl bromide is prepared by constant boiling with HBr (48%). For alkyl iodide, heat alcohols with sodium or potassium iodide in 95% ortho-phosphoric acid.

subject: Chemistry
text: Reactivity Order The reactivity of alcohols with haloacid follows this order: $$ 3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ} $$.
text_html:
Reactivity Order
The reactivity of alcohols with haloacid follows this order: $$ 3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ} $$.

subject: Chemistry
text: Phosphorus Halides In Situ Phosphorus tribromide and triiodide are generated in situ by the reaction of red phosphorus with bromine and iodine respectively.
text_html:
Phosphorus Halides In Situ
Phosphorus tribromide and triiodide are generated in situ by the reaction of red phosphorus with bromine and iodine respectively.

subject: Chemistry
text: Halogenation of Alkanes Free radical chlorination or bromination of alkanes yields a complex mixture of isomeric mono- and polyhaloalkanes that is hard to separate into pure compounds.
text_html:
Halogenation of Alkanes
Free radical chlorination or bromination of alkanes yields a complex mixture of isomeric mono- and polyhaloalkanes that is hard to separate into pure compounds.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alkene Halogenation - Hydrogen Halides Add hydrogen halides like HCl, HBr, or HI to alkenes to convert them to alkyl halides. Propene yields a major product following Markovnikov’s rule.
text_html:
Alkene Halogenation - Hydrogen Halides
Add hydrogen halides like HCl, HBr, or HI to alkenes to convert them to alkyl halides. Propene yields a major product following Markovnikov’s rule.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alkene Halogenation - Addition of Halogens Add bromine in CCl₄ to an alkene. The reddish-brown color discharges, indicating the detection of a double bond and creating colorless vic-dibromides.
text_html:
Alkene Halogenation - Addition of Halogens
Add bromine in CCl₄ to an alkene. The reddish-brown color discharges, indicating the detection of a double bond and creating colorless vic-dibromides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Finkelstein Reaction Alkyl iodides are produced by exchanging with alkyl chlorides or bromides using NaI in dry acetone. NaCl or NaBr formed precipitates, promoting forward reaction according to Le Chatelier's Principle.
text_html:
Finkelstein Reaction
Alkyl iodides are produced by exchanging with alkyl chlorides or bromides using NaI in dry acetone. NaCl or NaBr formed precipitates, promoting forward reaction according to Le Chatelier's Principle.

subject: Chemistry
text: Swarts Reaction Heating an alkyl chloride or bromide with metallic fluorides like AgF or SbF₃ produces alkyl fluorides.
text_html:
Swarts Reaction
Heating an alkyl chloride or bromide with metallic fluorides like AgF or SbF₃ produces alkyl fluorides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation of Alkyl Halides from Alcohols The hydroxyl group in alcohol is replaced by halogen using halogen acids, phosphorus halides, or thionyl chloride. Thionyl chloride is preferred due to the gaseous by-products $${SO}_2$$ and HCl, ensuring pure alkyl halides.
text_html:
Preparation of Alkyl Halides from Alcohols
The hydroxyl group in alcohol is replaced by halogen using halogen acids, phosphorus halides, or thionyl chloride. Thionyl chloride is preferred due to the gaseous by-products $${SO}_2$$ and HCl, ensuring pure alkyl halides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Reaction Conditions - Primary and secondary alcohols require a catalyst like {tex}ZnCl_2{/tex} when reacting with HCl. - Tertiary alcohols can react with concentrated HCl at room temperature.
text_html:
Reaction Conditions
- Primary and secondary alcohols require a catalyst like {tex}ZnCl_2{/tex} when reacting with HCl.
- Tertiary alcohols can react with concentrated HCl at room temperature.

subject: Chemistry
text: Using Phosphorus Halides - Alkyl halides can be produced using {tex}{RX}_3{/tex} reactions. - Phosphorus tribromide/triiodide are generated in situ via red phosphorus reacting with halogens like bromine or iodine.
text_html:
Using Phosphorus Halides
- Alkyl halides can be produced using {tex}{RX}_3{/tex} reactions.
- Phosphorus tribromide/triiodide are generated in situ via red phosphorus reacting with halogens like bromine or iodine.

subject: Chemistry
text: Order of Reactivity The reactivity order for alcohols with haloacids is tertiary > secondary > primary. This affects how quickly and efficiently the alcohols convert to the corresponding alkyl halides.
text_html:
Order of Reactivity
The reactivity order for alcohols with haloacids is tertiary > secondary > primary. This affects how quickly and efficiently the alcohols convert to the corresponding alkyl halides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Free Radical Halogenation of Alkanes - Chlorination or bromination leads to a mix of mono- and polyhaloalkanes. - The reaction isn't specific, making single compound yields low. Example reaction: {tex}{CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {CH}_3 ightarrow {CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {Cl} + {CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CHClCH}_3{/tex} .
text_html:
Free Radical Halogenation of Alkanes
- Chlorination or bromination leads to a mix of mono- and polyhaloalkanes.
- The reaction isn't specific, making single compound yields low. Example reaction: {tex}{CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {CH}_3 ightarrow {CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {CH}_2 {Cl} + {CH}_3 {CH}_2 {CHClCH}_3{/tex}.

subject: Chemistry
text: Alkyl Halide Preparation from Alkenes Hydrogen halides convert alkenes to alkyl halides. Example: Propene reacts to form two products, with one predominant according to Markovnikov's rule.
text_html:
Alkyl Halide Preparation from Alkenes
Hydrogen halides convert alkenes to alkyl halides. Example: Propene reacts to form two products, with one predominant according to Markovnikov's rule.

subject: Chemistry
text: Addition of Halogens to Alkenes - Bromine in CCl4 is added to identify double bonds by losing color. - This results in the formation of vic-dibromides.
text_html:
Addition of Halogens to Alkenes
- Bromine in CCl4 is added to identify double bonds by losing color.
- This results in the formation of vic-dibromides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Halogen Exchange via Finkelstein Reaction - Alkyl iodides result from reacting alkyl chlorides/bromides with NaI in dry acetone. Sodium halides precipitate out, promoting the forward reaction.
text_html:
Halogen Exchange via Finkelstein Reaction
- Alkyl iodides result from reacting alkyl chlorides/bromides with NaI in dry acetone. Sodium halides precipitate out, promoting the forward reaction.

subject: Chemistry
text: Le Chatelier's Principle This principle is applied in halogen exchange reactions. Removal of sodium _halide product facilitates the reaction, driving it forward.
text_html:
Le Chatelier's Principle
This principle is applied in halogen exchange reactions. Removal of sodium _halide product facilitates the reaction, driving it forward.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation of Alkyl Fluorides Alkyl fluorides are synthesized by heating alkyl chlorides/bromides with compounds like AgF, {tex}{Hg}_2F_2{/tex} , {tex}{CoF}_2{/tex} , or {tex}{SbF}_3{/tex} . This procedure is known as the Swarts reaction.
text_html:
Preparation of Alkyl Fluorides
Alkyl fluorides are synthesized by heating alkyl chlorides/bromides with compounds like AgF, {tex}{Hg}_2F_2{/tex}, {tex}{CoF}_2{/tex}, or {tex}{SbF}_3{/tex}. This procedure is known as the Swarts reaction.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation of Aryl Chlorides and Bromides Aryl chlorides and bromides can be synthesized through electrophilic substitution of arenes with chlorine or bromine.
text_html: Preparation of Aryl Chlorides and Bromides Aryl chlorides and bromides can be synthesized through electrophilic substitution of arenes with chlorine or bromine.

subject: Chemistry
text: Catalysts in Electrophilic Substitution The substitution reaction requires the presence of Lewis acid catalysts such as iron or iron(III) chloride.
text_html: Catalysts in Electrophilic Substitution The substitution reaction requires the presence of Lewis acid catalysts such as iron or iron(III) chloride.

subject: Chemistry
text: Isomer Separation The ortho and para isomers produced have significantly different melting points, allowing for easy separation.
text_html: Isomer Separation The ortho and para isomers produced have significantly different melting points, allowing for easy separation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Iodination Reactions - Iodination is reversible. - Requires an oxidizing agent like HNO3 or HIO4 to oxidize the HI byproduct. - Fluoro compounds are not produced this way due to fluorine's high reactivity.
text_html: Iodination Reactions - Iodination is reversible. - Requires an oxidizing agent like HNO3 or HIO4 to oxidize the HI byproduct. - Fluoro compounds are not produced this way due to fluorine's high reactivity.

subject: Chemistry
text: Sandmeyer’s Reaction with Amines - Primary aromatic amines react with sodium nitrite in cold aqueous mineral acid. - This forms a diazonium salt which can undergo group replacement.
text_html: Sandmeyer’s Reaction with Amines - Primary aromatic amines react with sodium nitrite in cold aqueous mineral acid. - This forms a diazonium salt which can undergo group replacement.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Cuprous Halides - The diazonium group in the salt can be replaced by -Cl or -Br using cuprous chloride or cuprous bromide.
text_html: Role of Cuprous Halides - The diazonium group in the salt can be replaced by -Cl or -Br using cuprous chloride or cuprous bromide.

subject: Chemistry
text: Formation of Diazonium Salt - Mixing the diazonium salt with cuprous halides allows substitution by chlorine or bromine.
text_html: Formation of Diazonium Salt - Mixing the diazonium salt with cuprous halides allows substitution by chlorine or bromine.

subject: Chemistry
text: Direct Iodine Replacement - Replacement with iodine does not require cuprous halides. - Simply shaking the diazonium salt with potassium iodide can achieve the replacement.
text_html: Direct Iodine Replacement - Replacement with iodine does not require cuprous halides. - Simply shaking the diazonium salt with potassium iodide can achieve the replacement.

subject: Chemistry
text: Visual Representation Refer to diagrams for molecular structures and reaction paths during electrophilic substitution and Sandmeyer's reactions.
text_html: Visual Representation Refer to diagrams for molecular structures and reaction paths during electrophilic substitution and Sandmeyer's reactions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Applications of Electrophilic Substitution - Utilized in the organic synthesis of various aromatic halides with specific functional purposes.
text_html: Applications of Electrophilic Substitution - Utilized in the organic synthesis of various aromatic halides with specific functional purposes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Preparation of Aryl Chlorides and Bromides Aryl chlorides and bromides are produced by electrophilic substitution using arenes with chlorine and bromine. This process requires the presence of Lewis acid catalysts like iron or iron(III) chloride.
text_html:
Preparation of Aryl Chlorides and Bromides
Aryl chlorides and bromides are produced by electrophilic substitution using arenes with chlorine and bromine. This process requires the presence of Lewis acid catalysts like iron or iron(III) chloride.

subject: Chemistry
text: Melting Points Aid Separation Ortho and para isomers of aryl chlorides and bromides are separated due to their significantly different melting points.
text_html:
Melting Points Aid Separation
Ortho and para isomers of aryl chlorides and bromides are separated due to their significantly different melting points.

subject: Chemistry
text: Iodination Requires Oxidizing Agents Iodine reactions are reversible, so they need oxidizing agents such as HNO3 or HIO4 to counteract the effect of HI formed during iodination.
text_html:
Iodination Requires Oxidizing Agents
Iodine reactions are reversible, so they need oxidizing agents such as HNO3 or HIO4 to counteract the effect of HI formed during iodination.

subject: Chemistry
text: Exception for Fluoro Compounds Fluoro compounds are not synthesized via electrophilic substitution because of the high reactivity of fluorine.
text_html:
Exception for Fluoro Compounds
Fluoro compounds are not synthesized via electrophilic substitution because of the high reactivity of fluorine.

subject: Chemistry
text: Sandmeyer's Reaction: Formation of Diazonium Salt Primary aromatic amines are converted to diazonium salts by treating with sodium nitrite in cold aqueous mineral acid.
text_html:
Sandmeyer's Reaction: Formation of Diazonium Salt
Primary aromatic amines are converted to diazonium salts by treating with sodium nitrite in cold aqueous mineral acid.

subject: Chemistry
text: Replacement of Diazonium Group The diazonium group can be replaced with chlorine or bromine by mixing it with cuprous chloride or cuprous bromide.
text_html:
Replacement of Diazonium Group
The diazonium group can be replaced with chlorine or bromine by mixing it with cuprous chloride or cuprous bromide.

subject: Chemistry
text: Iodine Substitution Simplified To replace the diazonium group with iodine, simply shake the diazonium salt with potassium iodide. Cuprous halide is unnecessary.
text_html:
Iodine Substitution Simplified
To replace the diazonium group with iodine, simply shake the diazonium salt with potassium iodide. Cuprous halide is unnecessary.

subject: Chemistry
text: Lewis Acid Catalysts for Electrophilic Substitution Iron and iron(III) chloride are commonly used as Lewis acid catalysts to facilitate reactions of arenes with chlorine or bromine.
text_html:
Lewis Acid Catalysts for Electrophilic Substitution
Iron and iron(III) chloride are commonly used as Lewis acid catalysts to facilitate reactions of arenes with chlorine or bromine.

subject: Chemistry
text: Reactivity Differences in Halogenation The ease of reactions involves fluorine's high reactivity, differing greatly from that of chlorine, bromine, and iodine.
text_html:
Reactivity Differences in Halogenation
The ease of reactions involves fluorine's high reactivity, differing greatly from that of chlorine, bromine, and iodine.

subject: Chemistry
text: Concept Review: Aryl Compounds via Electrophilic Substitution Understanding electrophilic substitution mechanisms and catalyst roles is crucial for synthesizing aryl chlorides, bromides, and iodides effectively.
text_html:
Concept Review: Aryl Compounds via Electrophilic Substitution
Understanding electrophilic substitution mechanisms and catalyst roles is crucial for synthesizing aryl chlorides, bromides, and iodides effectively.

subject: Chemistry
text: Properties of Alkyl Halides Alkyl halides are colorless when pure, but bromides and iodides develop color upon exposure to light. Many volatile halogen compounds emit a sweet smell.
text_html:
Properties of Alkyl Halides
Alkyl halides are colorless when pure, but bromides and iodides develop color upon exposure to light. Many volatile halogen compounds emit a sweet smell.

subject: Chemistry
text: Melting and Boiling Points Gases at room temperature include methyl chloride, methyl bromide, ethyl chloride, and some chlorofluoromethanes. Larger molecules are liquids or solids.
text_html:
Melting and Boiling Points
Gases at room temperature include methyl chloride, methyl bromide, ethyl chloride, and some chlorofluoromethanes. Larger molecules are liquids or solids.

subject: Chemistry
text: Impact of Molecular Structure Molecules in organic halogen compounds display higher intermolecular forces (dipole-dipole and van der Waals) due to greater polarity and mass compared to hydrocarbons, resulting in higher boiling points.
text_html:
Impact of Molecular Structure
Molecules in organic halogen compounds display higher intermolecular forces (dipole-dipole and van der Waals) due to greater polarity and mass compared to hydrocarbons, resulting in higher boiling points.

subject: Chemistry
text: Trend in Boiling Points Boiling points decrease in the order: RI > RBr > RCl > RF. This is due to increased van der Waals forces associated with larger halogen atoms.
text_html:
Trend in Boiling Points
Boiling points decrease in the order: RI > RBr > RCl > RF. This is due to increased van der Waals forces associated with larger halogen atoms.

subject: Chemistry
text: Isomeric Variations Boiling points of isomeric haloalkanes decrease with increased branching. For para-isomers in dihalobenzenes, melting points are higher due to better symmetry fitting in crystal lattices.
text_html:
Isomeric Variations
Boiling points of isomeric haloalkanes decrease with increased branching. For para-isomers in dihalobenzenes, melting points are higher due to better symmetry fitting in crystal lattices.

subject: Chemistry
text: Density Considerations Bromo, iodo, and polychloro derivatives are heavier than water. Density increases with the number and mass of carbon and halogen atoms.
text_html:
Density Considerations
Bromo, iodo, and polychloro derivatives are heavier than water. Density increases with the number and mass of carbon and halogen atoms.

subject: Chemistry
text: Density Examples The density of various haloalkanes varies, and is calculated based on the number and type of halogen and carbon atoms involved.
text_html:
Density Examples
The density of various haloalkanes varies, and is calculated based on the number and type of halogen and carbon atoms involved.

subject: Chemistry
text: Solubility in Water Haloalkanes are minimally soluble in water. Dissolving them requires breaking existing attractions stronger than those formed with water.
text_html:
Solubility in Water
Haloalkanes are minimally soluble in water. Dissolving them requires breaking existing attractions stronger than those formed with water.

subject: Chemistry
text: Solubility in Organic Solvents Haloalkanes tend to dissolve in organic solvents where the intermolecular attractions formed are similar to those broken in each separate compound.
text_html:
Solubility in Organic Solvents
Haloalkanes tend to dissolve in organic solvents where the intermolecular attractions formed are similar to those broken in each separate compound.

subject: Chemistry
text: Visual Patterns Figures and tables in literature often represent variations in boiling points and densities of alkyl halides for comparative analysis.
text_html:
Visual Patterns
Figures and tables in literature often represent variations in boiling points and densities of alkyl halides for comparative analysis.

subject: Chemistry
text: Basic Properties of Alkyl Halides Alkyl halides are typically colorless in their pure form. It's interesting to note that bromides and iodides can develop color upon exposure to light.
text_html:
Basic Properties of Alkyl Halides
Alkyl halides are typically colorless in their pure form. It's interesting to note that bromides and iodides can develop color upon exposure to light.

subject: Chemistry
text: Volatility and Odor Numerous volatile halogen compounds possess a distinct sweet smell.
text_html:
Volatility and Odor
Numerous volatile halogen compounds possess a distinct sweet smell.

subject: Chemistry
text: Physical States of Various Halides - Methyl chloride, methyl bromide, ethyl chloride, and some chlorofluoromethanes remain gases at room temperature. - Halides with higher molecular mass can exist as either liquids or solids.
text_html:
Physical States of Various Halides
- Methyl chloride, methyl bromide, ethyl chloride, and some chlorofluoromethanes remain gases at room temperature.
- Halides with higher molecular mass can exist as either liquids or solids.

subject: Chemistry
text: Polarity of Organic Halogen Compounds These molecules are generally polar, resulting in stronger intermolecular forces, such as dipole-dipole and van der Waals forces, when compared to their hydrocarbon counterparts.
text_html:
Polarity of Organic Halogen Compounds
These molecules are generally polar, resulting in stronger intermolecular forces, such as dipole-dipole and van der Waals forces, when compared to their hydrocarbon counterparts.

subject: Chemistry
text: Comparison of Boiling Points - Due to the greater intermolecular forces, halogen derivatives have significantly higher boiling points than hydrocarbons of similar molecular mass. - Boiling points of alkyl halides, for the same alkyl group, follow this order: RI > RBr > RCl > RF.
text_html:
Comparison of Boiling Points
- Due to the greater intermolecular forces, halogen derivatives have significantly higher boiling points than hydrocarbons of similar molecular mass.
- Boiling points of alkyl halides, for the same alkyl group, follow this order: RI > RBr > RCl > RF.

subject: Chemistry
text: Effect of Molecular Size on Boiling Points As molecular size and electron count increase, the strength of intermolecular attractions also increases, further influencing boiling points.
text_html:
Effect of Molecular Size on Boiling Points
As molecular size and electron count increase, the strength of intermolecular attractions also increases, further influencing boiling points.

subject: Chemistry
text: Isomeric Effects on Boiling Points - For isomeric haloalkanes, increased branching tends to lower the boiling point. - 2-bromo-2-methylpropane is an example of an isomer with a comparatively low boiling point.
text_html:
Isomeric Effects on Boiling Points
- For isomeric haloalkanes, increased branching tends to lower the boiling point.
- 2-bromo-2-methylpropane is an example of an isomer with a comparatively low boiling point.

subject: Chemistry
text: Boiling Points of Isomeric Dihalobenzenes - Most of these isomers have nearly the same boiling points. - Notably, para-isomers exhibit a higher melting point due to their symmetrical arrangement fitting better in crystal lattices compared to ortho and meta-isomers.
text_html:
Boiling Points of Isomeric Dihalobenzenes
- Most of these isomers have nearly the same boiling points.
- Notably, para-isomers exhibit a higher melting point due to their symmetrical arrangement fitting better in crystal lattices compared to ortho and meta-isomers.

subject: Chemistry
text: Bromo, Iodo, and Polychloro Density - These derivatives tend to be denser than water. - Density tends to increase with more carbon atoms, halogen atoms, and increased atomic mass of halogens involved.
text_html:
Bromo, Iodo, and Polychloro Density
- These derivatives tend to be denser than water.
- Density tends to increase with more carbon atoms, halogen atoms, and increased atomic mass of halogens involved.

subject: Chemistry
text: Solubility Dynamics of Haloalkanes - Haloalkanes exhibit low solubility in water, due to the weaker intermolecular attractions set up in comparison to the strong hydrogen bonds present in water. - However, they dissolve more readily in organic solvents, where intermolecular attraction strengths are compatible.
text_html:
Solubility Dynamics of Haloalkanes
- Haloalkanes exhibit low solubility in water, due to the weaker intermolecular attractions set up in comparison to the strong hydrogen bonds present in water.
- However, they dissolve more readily in organic solvents, where intermolecular attraction strengths are compatible.

subject: Chemistry
text: S N 1 S N 2 Carbon (sp 3 ) Electrophile Favored by 3 o alkyl halides or benzylic, or allylic can also react by an S N 1 mechanism (More stable carbocation) Favored by = CH 3 and 1 o alkyl halides (less sterically hindered) Nucleophile Nature of the nucleophile has no affect on rate. In general, S N 1 use neutral, weak nucleophiles Favored by more reactive nucleophiles RS - > NC - > RO - > HO - > Cl - Solvent Effect Favored by polar, protic solvents. Favored by polar, aprotic solvents.
text_html:
SN1 SN2
Carbon (sp3) Electrophile Favored by 3o alkyl halides or benzylic, or allylic can also react by an SN1 mechanism (More stable carbocation) Favored by = CH3 and 1o alkyl halides
(less sterically hindered)
Nucleophile Nature of the nucleophile has no affect on rate. In general, SN1 use neutral, weak nucleophiles Favored by more reactive nucleophiles
RS- > NC- > RO- > HO- > Cl-
Solvent Effect Favored by polar, protic solvents. Favored by polar, aprotic solvents.

subject: Chemistry
text: Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions Nucleophiles are electron-rich species that attack electron-deficient areas of a substrate, such as haloalkanes. The nucleophile swaps places with a halogen in the molecule, resulting in a nucleophilic substitution reaction.
text_html:
Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions
Nucleophiles are electron-rich species that attack electron-deficient areas of a substrate, such as haloalkanes. The nucleophile swaps places with a halogen in the molecule, resulting in a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

subject: Chemistry
text: Nucleophilic Substitution Mechanism SN2 Mechanism: A second-order kinematic reaction where the nucleophile displaces the dislodging halogen in one synchronous step. SN1 Mechanism: Involves a two-step process with an intermediate carbocation; often occurs in polar solvents.
text_html:
Nucleophilic Substitution Mechanism
  • SN2 Mechanism: A second-order kinematic reaction where the nucleophile displaces the dislodging halogen in one synchronous step.
  • SN1 Mechanism: Involves a two-step process with an intermediate carbocation; often occurs in polar solvents.

subject: Chemistry
text: Ambident Nucleophiles Nucleophiles like cyanides and nitrites can bond through two different atoms, making them ambident. For example, cyanides can attach through either the carbon or nitrogen atom.
text_html:
Ambident Nucleophiles
Nucleophiles like cyanides and nitrites can bond through two different atoms, making them ambident. For example, cyanides can attach through either the carbon or nitrogen atom.

subject: Chemistry
text: Order of Reactivity SN2 Reactions: Methyl > Primary > Secondary > Tertiary halides. SN1 Reactions: Favors tertiary carbocations due to their stability. Alkyl Halides: Reactivity: iodides > bromides > chlorides >> fluorides.
text_html:
Order of Reactivity
  • SN2 Reactions: Methyl > Primary > Secondary > Tertiary halides.
  • SN1 Reactions: Favors tertiary carbocations due to their stability.
  • Alkyl Halides: Reactivity: iodides > bromides > chlorides >> fluorides.

subject: Chemistry
text: Stereochemical Aspects Focus on the understanding of optical activity, chirality, retention, inversion, and racemisation to assess changes in molecular structure during reactions.
text_html:
Stereochemical Aspects
Focus on the understanding of optical activity, chirality, retention, inversion, and racemisation to assess changes in molecular structure during reactions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Optical Isomerism Compounds rotating plane-polarized light are optically active. Dextrorotatory rotates light to the right, laevorotatory to the left. Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images.
text_html:
Optical Isomerism
Compounds rotating plane-polarized light are optically active. Dextrorotatory rotates light to the right, laevorotatory to the left. Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images.

subject: Chemistry
text: Elimination Reactions In elimination processes, a beta hydrogen and alpha halogen atom are removed, forming an alkene. Known as beta-elimination; follows Zaitsev's Rule for major product formation.
text_html:
Elimination Reactions
In elimination processes, a beta hydrogen and alpha halogen atom are removed, forming an alkene. Known as beta-elimination; follows Zaitsev's Rule for major product formation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Competing Reactions Haloalkanes can undergo substitution or elimination based on the type of solvent or nucleophile used. Bulkier bases favor elimination over identical conditions supporting substitution.
text_html:
Competing Reactions
Haloalkanes can undergo substitution or elimination based on the type of solvent or nucleophile used. Bulkier bases favor elimination over identical conditions supporting substitution.

subject: Chemistry
text: Reaction with Metals Haloalkanes react with metals like magnesium in dry ether to form organo-metallic compounds (e.g., Grignard Reagents, RMgX). They are used for synthesizing hydrocarbons.
text_html:
Reaction with Metals
Haloalkanes react with metals like magnesium in dry ether to form organo-metallic compounds (e.g., Grignard Reagents, RMgX). They are used for synthesizing hydrocarbons.

subject: Chemistry
text: Wurtz Reaction In this reaction, haloalkanes react with sodium in dry ether to yield alkanes with an increased carbon count.
text_html:
Wurtz Reaction
In this reaction, haloalkanes react with sodium in dry ether to yield alkanes with an increased carbon count.

subject: Chemistry
text: Definition of Haloarenes: Haloarenes are aromatic compounds where one or more hydrogen atoms of benzene are replaced by halogen atoms (Cl, Br, I, F).
text_html: Definition of Haloarenes: Haloarenes are aromatic compounds where one or more hydrogen atoms of benzene are replaced by halogen atoms (Cl, Br, I, F).

subject: Chemistry
text: Physical State: - Lower haloarenes like chlorobenzene and bromobenzene are colorless liquids at room temperature. - Higher haloarenes with more halogen atoms are typically solids.
text_html: Physical State: - Lower haloarenes like chlorobenzene and bromobenzene are colorless liquids at room temperature. - Higher haloarenes with more halogen atoms are typically solids.

subject: Chemistry
text: Boiling Points: - Haloarenes have higher boiling points than benzene. - This is due to the polar C–X bond creating dipole-dipole attractions and a higher molecular mass. - Order of boiling points for the same number of halogen atoms: * Iodobenzene > Bromobenzene > Chlorobenzene > Fluorobenzene.
text_html: Boiling Points: - Haloarenes have higher boiling points than benzene. - This is due to the polar C–X bond creating dipole-dipole attractions and a higher molecular mass. - Order of boiling points for the same number of halogen atoms: * Iodobenzene > Bromobenzene > Chlorobenzene > Fluorobenzene.

subject: Chemistry
text: Melting Points: - Melting points vary based on the molecular structure. - Para-isomers, such as p-dichlorobenzene, have higher melting points than ortho and meta isomers due to their symmetrical structure that allows better packing in the solid state.
text_html: Melting Points: - Melting points vary based on the molecular structure. - Para-isomers, such as p-dichlorobenzene, have higher melting points than ortho and meta isomers due to their symmetrical structure that allows better packing in the solid state.

subject: Chemistry
text: Solubility: - Haloarenes are insoluble in water because they cannot form hydrogen bonds with it. - However, they are soluble in organic solvents like benzene, ether, and chloroform.
text_html: Solubility: - Haloarenes are insoluble in water because they cannot form hydrogen bonds with it. - However, they are soluble in organic solvents like benzene, ether, and chloroform.

subject: Chemistry
text: Density: - Haloarenes are denser than water due to the high atomic mass of halogen atoms. - Density order is Iodobenzene > Bromobenzene > Chlorobenzene.
text_html: Density: - Haloarenes are denser than water due to the high atomic mass of halogen atoms. - Density order is Iodobenzene > Bromobenzene > Chlorobenzene.

subject: Chemistry
text: Polar C–X bond: - The presence of the polar carbon-halogen bond is a key factor for the higher boiling points of haloarenes when compared to benzene.
text_html: Polar C–X bond: - The presence of the polar carbon-halogen bond is a key factor for the higher boiling points of haloarenes when compared to benzene.

subject: Chemistry
text: Effect of Molecular Mass: - Increased molecular mass contributes to the elevated boiling points of haloarenes in comparison to benzene.
text_html: Effect of Molecular Mass: - Increased molecular mass contributes to the elevated boiling points of haloarenes in comparison to benzene.

subject: Chemistry
text: Symmetry of Para-isomers: - The symmetry in para-isomers like p-dichlorobenzene enhances their melting point due to better packing capabilities in their solid form.
text_html: Symmetry of Para-isomers: - The symmetry in para-isomers like p-dichlorobenzene enhances their melting point due to better packing capabilities in their solid form.

subject: Chemistry
text: General Characteristics: - Haloarenes involve the replacement of hydrogen by halogens in benzene. - They exhibit unique physical properties such as varied boiling and melting points, solubility, and density characteristics.
text_html: General Characteristics: - Haloarenes involve the replacement of hydrogen by halogens in benzene. - They exhibit unique physical properties such as varied boiling and melting points, solubility, and density characteristics.

subject: Chemistry
text: Nucleophilic Substitution in Aryl Halides Aryl halides show less reactivity towards nucleophilic substitutions compared to alkyl halides due to resonance and hybridization effects.
text_html:
Nucleophilic Substitution in Aryl Halides
Aryl halides show less reactivity towards nucleophilic substitutions compared to alkyl halides due to resonance and hybridization effects.

subject: Chemistry
text: Resonance Effect In haloarenes, electron pairs on halogen atoms resonate with the benzene ring, limiting bond cleavage and reducing reactivity.
text_html:
Resonance Effect
In haloarenes, electron pairs on halogen atoms resonate with the benzene ring, limiting bond cleavage and reducing reactivity.

subject: Chemistry
text: Carbon Hybridization The sp2 hybridization of carbon in haloarenes holds the C-X bond stronger, contributing to lower reactivity than the sp3 of haloalkanes.
text_html:
Carbon Hybridization
The sp2 hybridization of carbon in haloarenes holds the C-X bond stronger, contributing to lower reactivity than the sp3 of haloalkanes.

subject: Chemistry
text: Phenyl Cation Instability Phenyl cations in haloarenes lack resonance stability, ruling out SN1 reactions and limiting substitution reactivity.
text_html:
Phenyl Cation Instability
Phenyl cations in haloarenes lack resonance stability, ruling out SN1 reactions and limiting substitution reactivity.

subject: Chemistry
text: Role of Electron-Withdrawing Groups Electron-withdrawing groups like -NO2 enhance reactivity when positioned ortho or para. No effect at the meta-position.
text_html:
Role of Electron-Withdrawing Groups
  • Electron-withdrawing groups like -NO2 enhance reactivity when positioned ortho or para.
  • No effect at the meta-position.

subject: Chemistry
text: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions Haloarenes go through typical benzene electrophilic reactions, but condition severity is higher due to the deactivating nature of halogens.
text_html:
Electrophilic Substitution Reactions
Haloarenes go through typical benzene electrophilic reactions, but condition severity is higher due to the deactivating nature of halogens.

subject: Chemistry
text: Directing Influence of Halogens Halogens orient electrophilic substitution towards ortho and para positions, increasing electron density from resonance.
text_html:
Directing Influence of Halogens
Halogens orient electrophilic substitution towards ortho and para positions, increasing electron density from resonance.

subject: Chemistry
text: Effect of Halogen's -I Effect The halogen atom withdraws electrons, slightly deactivating the benzene ring and demanding drastic reaction conditions.
text_html:
Effect of Halogen's -I Effect
The halogen atom withdraws electrons, slightly deactivating the benzene ring and demanding drastic reaction conditions.

subject: Chemistry
text: Wurtz-Fittig Reaction Aryl and alkyl halides react with sodium in dry ether to form alkylarenes, known as the Wurtz-Fittig reaction.
text_html:
Wurtz-Fittig Reaction
Aryl and alkyl halides react with sodium in dry ether to form alkylarenes, known as the Wurtz-Fittig reaction.

subject: Chemistry
text: Fittig Reaction Aryl halides react with sodium in dry ether, joining aryl groups, forming products via the Fittig reaction.
text_html:
Fittig Reaction
Aryl halides react with sodium in dry ether, joining aryl groups, forming products via the Fittig reaction.

subject: Chemistry
text: Polyhalogen Compounds Carbon compounds with more than one halogen atom are known as polyhalogen compounds and are used in numerous industrial and agricultural applications.
text_html:
Polyhalogen Compounds
Carbon compounds with more than one halogen atom are known as polyhalogen compounds and are used in numerous industrial and agricultural applications.

subject: Chemistry
text: Dichloromethane (Methylene Chloride) Dichloromethane is a solvent used in paint removers and aerosol propellants. It can harm the central nervous system and affect hearing and vision. Exposure results in dizziness, nausea, and skin irritation.
text_html:
Dichloromethane (Methylene Chloride)
Dichloromethane is a solvent used in paint removers and aerosol propellants. It can harm the central nervous system and affect hearing and vision. Exposure results in dizziness, nausea, and skin irritation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Trichloromethane (Chloroform) Previously used as a surgical anesthetic, chloroform mainly produces the refrigerant R-22 today. It can harm the liver and kidneys and has a toxic gas byproduct, phosgene. Chloroform needs dark storage to prevent oxidation.
text_html:
Trichloromethane (Chloroform)
Previously used as a surgical anesthetic, chloroform mainly produces the refrigerant R-22 today. It can harm the liver and kidneys and has a toxic gas byproduct, phosgene. Chloroform needs dark storage to prevent oxidation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Triiodomethane (Iodoform) Historically an antiseptic due to free iodine release, iodoform's strong smell has led to replacement by other iodine formulations.
text_html:
Triiodomethane (Iodoform)
Historically an antiseptic due to free iodine release, iodoform's strong smell has led to replacement by other iodine formulations.

subject: Chemistry
text: Tetrachloromethane (Carbon Tetrachloride) Important in creating refrigerants and as a cleaning agent until the 1960s. It depletes the ozone layer, leading to increased UV exposure, affects the liver, and can cause harmful nerve damage and heart irregularities.
text_html:
Tetrachloromethane (Carbon Tetrachloride)
Important in creating refrigerants and as a cleaning agent until the 1960s. It depletes the ozone layer, leading to increased UV exposure, affects the liver, and can cause harmful nerve damage and heart irregularities.

subject: Chemistry
text: Freons Chlorofluorocarbons such as Freon-12, used in refrigerants and aerosols, are stable and inert but deplete the ozone layer, leading to chain reactions in the stratosphere.
text_html:
Freons
Chlorofluorocarbons such as Freon-12, used in refrigerants and aerosols, are stable and inert but deplete the ozone layer, leading to chain reactions in the stratosphere.

subject: Chemistry
text: p,p’-Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane (DDT) Developed in 1873 but gained fame post-1939 as an insecticide for malaria control. Nobel Prize awarded to Paul Muller in 1948. DDT is toxic, accumulates in fatty tissues, and was banned in the US in 1973 due to ecological impacts.
text_html:
p,p’-Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane (DDT)
Developed in 1873 but gained fame post-1939 as an insecticide for malaria control. Nobel Prize awarded to Paul Muller in 1948. DDT is toxic, accumulates in fatty tissues, and was banned in the US in 1973 due to ecological impacts.

subject: Chemistry
text: Health Risks of Methylene Chloride May affect the human central nervous system leading to dizziness and vision problems. Causes skin burns and redness upon contact.
text_html:
Health Risks of Methylene Chloride
May affect the human central nervous system leading to dizziness and vision problems. Causes skin burns and redness upon contact.

subject: Chemistry
text: Chloroform's Risks and Storage Toxic to kidneys and liver, causes dizziness, and must be stored in dark, airtight containers to prevent hazardous gas formation.
text_html:
Chloroform's Risks and Storage
Toxic to kidneys and liver, causes dizziness, and must be stored in dark, airtight containers to prevent hazardous gas formation.

subject: Chemistry
text: Environmental Impact of Carbon Tetrachloride It affects the ozone layer, increasing risks of skin and eye diseases through heightened UV exposure. Potentially causes liver cancer and has immediate effects like dizziness and nausea.
text_html:
Environmental Impact of Carbon Tetrachloride
It affects the ozone layer, increasing risks of skin and eye diseases through heightened UV exposure. Potentially causes liver cancer and has immediate effects like dizziness and nausea.
